speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number eight. Survivor and war crimes trial participant, Lawrence Rhee, and the date is April 9, 1996. Can you tell us about these pictures, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: These pictures, again, are in the two volumes that the Russian prosecution staff gave to me. The babies here are from two to three-- from two to three years old. One only seems to be months old. They're undernourished. They-- The one on the left has whooping cough and third-degree, I guess, hunger-- dying of hunger, or dystrophy. And the one in the middle shows a baby that is bleeding from the stomach because of the malnutrition. And the one on the right, again, is a case of third-degree dystrophy. The last one is 12 years old. The one in the middle is four years old. And the one in the left is two years old. This group of pictures are-- are frames taken out of a film that a German amateur military person took at the entrance to one of the concentration camps. The whole film lasted only about 90 seconds, or two minutes. And these, as I mentioned before, are some of the stills of the frames taken out of it. And you can see for yourself the treatment these people received at the entrance to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were asked to make some stills, of-- make-- make some frames from the film. And, again, I got an extra.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this introduced as evidence?

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any idea where this film is today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can suppose only that it is with the evidence at the Nuremberg trial-- where the Nuremberg trial records are being kept. Just where that is, I don't know. This is a copy of the indictment against the accused at the Nuremberg trial. And it-- during-- throughout the pages, it-- it-- it states the accusations against the various individuals on trial. This is my ID to get into the trial building, into the court building at Nuremberg. That's all I can say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you need to present this every time you went into the building?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had to-- had to show it each time. This is the English translation of the German propaganda film, I myself translated part of this during my time in photographic evidence. This is an [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] album, or catalog of the court cartoons done by Peis, who was a cartoonist, and who did all the cartoons in this-- of the-- of the accused in this album. This catalog was distributed during the Nuremberg trials. It was done in the Dachau concentration camp by the American Seventh Army Signal Corps. And I was fortunate enough to get a hold of one of the catalogs, or albums.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this given to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning of the trials. During the trials, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. This Nazi flag has some special memories for me. During the war, when we were advancing through Western Germany, we got a tip that, at the occasion of Hitler's 54th-- which should-- which would turn out to be his last birthday, some underground SS units intended to rise up behind our lines and cause difficulties by blowing up bridges, and so forth and so on. A German communist gave us the information. We, of course, followed it up. And pretty soon, we had two of the leaders in custody. But a third one [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was not home. We couldn't get him. In the meantime, we also found out that these SS group or groups had a case of arms hidden in some wooded area. It didn't take us long to find this, and we put some PW's Prisoners of War, today. Then, again, I went back to the house of the third person, and I finally found him. And without saying anything, I pulled out my .45, and told him to walk 10 steps ahead of me, and that he would know exactly where to walk-- where to go. And if he would make any wrong movement, I'd drill him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he went ahead, and led me directly to this case of arms. We had-- they had bazookas-- anti-tank guns, in other words-- and rifles, ammunition, and among other things, dynamite caps, and this flag.

speaker: interviewee
text: This sword was turned in as an arm. All Germans were required to turn in all kinds of arms they had in their homes. And somebody turned this in. And I was fortunate enough to see it first, so I kept it. That's about all I can say. It's-- but it was a very welcome souvenir.
